verbatim_text: And finally, in the self model section, we saw that we could train models to faithfully explain their internal computations. So near the end of that section, I framed self models as a path to better interpretability methods, which address key scalability, generality, and usability issues. But I think there's still a lot more open problems we want to address in each of these three categories to actually like realize this in the real world. So for scalability, a key challenge is how do we scalably generate supervision with a reasonable cost? Right. So when we train models to explain themselves, their mechanisms might actually shift over the course of training. So we might actually need to regenerate data or do some sort of like on policy training. So we want some scalable way of refreshing labels. But most interrupt tools either take a lot of manual engineering or large scale search. So we want to make supervision generation less expensive. Closely tied to this, we also want to have generalization across tasks. We don't want to train on every sort of self description question that we'd want to ask. So there might be something here that's analogous to like self supervised pre-training that might encourage task general self modeling. And I think finally, we'd want to validate that the self descriptions that models produce are actually useful for people on end tasks.
